channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: The current and future landscape of AI foundation models for cancer management
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72576-5

AI foundation models (FMs) are transforming cancer management and research. Here, we analyze the state-of-the-art FMs, discuss their impact on cancer management, and argue that the next generation of cancer AI FMs will be defined by multimodality, enhanced reasoning, maximized openness, and sustained human guidance.
item.dc:title: The current and future landscape of AI foundation models for cancer management
item.dc:creator: Chuang Niu, Ge Wang
Ge Wang
item.description:
Authors: Chuang Niu, Ge Wang

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Chuang Niu, Ge Wang

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72576-5 AI foundation models (FMs) are transforming cancer management and research. Here, we analyze the state-of-the-art FMs, discuss their impact on cancer management, and argue that the next generation of cancer AI FMs will be defined by multimodality, enhanced reasoning, maximized openness, and sustained human guidance.

item.title: Advancing environmental sustainability research and policy in sport
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72583-6

Sport holds profound global social influence, yet its systemic environmental footprint is often overlooked in sustainable development governance. Although environmental initiatives have proliferated in professional sport, many remain limited to public relations—fragmented, symbolic, and inadequate to tackle structural challenges. To facilitate a transition, a scientific framework is needed that offers coherent, verifiable pathways, bridging the gap between isolated sport initiatives and the broader systems of global environmental governance.
item.dc:title: Advancing environmental sustainability research and policy in sport
item.dc:creator: Yueting Zhang, Han Hao, Yikeranmu Yi Ming, Yaomin Zhang, Bing Shi
Han Hao
Yikeranmu Yi Ming
Yaomin Zhang
Bing Shi

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72583-6 Sport holds profound global social influence, yet its systemic environmental footprint is often overlooked in sustainable development governance. Although environmental initiatives have proliferated in professional sport, many remain limited to public relations—fragmented, symbolic, and inadequate to tackle structural challenges. To facilitate a transition, a scientific framework is needed that offers coherent, verifiable pathways, bridging the gap between isolated sport initiatives and the broader systems of global environmental governance.

item.title: FMOPhore for hotspot identification and efficient fragment-to-lead growth strategies
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72384-x

The study introduces FMOPhore, a computational tool that identifies key binding site residues to improve drug design by guiding efficient fragment growth and lead optimization across diverse protein targets.
item.dc:title: FMOPhore for hotspot identification and efficient fragment-to-lead growth strategies
item.dc:creator: Peter E. G. F. Ibrahim, Simone Altmann, Ulrich Zachariae, David Horn, Ian H. Gilbert, Michael J. Bodkin
Simone Altmann
Ulrich Zachariae
David Horn
Ian H. Gilbert
Michael J. Bodkin

Authors: Peter E. G. F. Ibrahim, Simone Altmann, Ulrich Zachariae, David Horn, Ian H. Gilbert, Michael J. Bodkin

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Ian H. Gilbert, Michael J. Bodkin

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72384-x The study introduces FMOPhore, a computational tool that identifies key binding site residues to improve drug design by guiding efficient fragment growth and lead optimization across diverse protein targets.

item.title: Optically reconfigurable physical unclonable functions based on 2D MoS<sub>2</sub> ring-oscillator arrays for attack-resistant hardware authentication
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72557-8

Conventional Si-based physical unclonable functions (PUFs) are normally limited by limited entropy and lack of reconfigurability, exposing them to machine learning attacks. Here, the authors report optically reconfigurable PUFs based on 2D MoS2 ring-oscillator arrays, demonstrating machine-learning-attack-resistant image encryption.
item.dc:title: Optically reconfigurable physical unclonable functions based on 2D MoS<sub>2</sub> ring-oscillator arrays for attack-resistant hardware authentication
item.dc:creator: Tong Li, Yuchao Zhou, Xingchao Zhang, et al.; last authors: Rong Yang, Guangyu Zhang

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72557-8 Conventional Si-based physical unclonable functions (PUFs) are normally limited by limited entropy and lack of reconfigurability, exposing them to machine learning attacks. Here, the authors report optically reconfigurable PUFs based on 2D MoS2 ring-oscillator arrays, demonstrating machine-learning-attack-resistant image encryption.

item.title: Accurate profiling of single-cell alternative transcript start sites by correcting RNA degradation
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72298-8

Alternative transcription start sites generate transcript diversity but are difficult to detect due to RNA degradation in 5’ scRNA-seq. Here, the authors present scATS, which corrects degradation to quantify TSS usage, improve cell clustering, and identify lung cancer-promoting TSS isoforms.
item.dc:title: Accurate profiling of single-cell alternative transcript start sites by correcting RNA degradation
item.dc:creator: Zijie Xu, Zhen Zhou, Chao Tang, et al.; last authors: Jing-wen Lin, Lu Chen

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72298-8 Alternative transcription start sites generate transcript diversity but are difficult to detect due to RNA degradation in 5’ scRNA-seq. Here, the authors present scATS, which corrects degradation to quantify TSS usage, improve cell clustering, and identify lung cancer-promoting TSS isoforms.

item.title: Artificial plateau neurons with in-situ spike-malleability for rhythmic quadrupedal locomotion
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72428-2

The authors report an artificial plateau neuron with bistable, spike-malleable dynamics that generates programmable rhythmic bursts, enabling a quadruped robot to walk adaptively and energy-efficiently via distributed hardware control without centralized computation.
item.dc:title: Artificial plateau neurons with in-situ spike-malleability for rhythmic quadrupedal locomotion
item.dc:creator: Hailiang Wang, Yishu Zhang, Qingao Chai, et al.; last authors: Yang Xu, Bin Yu

item.title: Atomic structures of medin and Aβ fibrils reveal polymorphic remodeling in mixed amyloid systems
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72515-4

Authors solve structures of in vitro Aβ40:medin mixtures to show three fibril populations and association within co-assemblies. Results support two non-exclusive mechanisms: transient interactions redirecting Aβ folding or partial medin incorporation into fibril assembly.
item.dc:title: Atomic structures of medin and Aβ fibrils reveal polymorphic remodeling in mixed amyloid systems
item.dc:creator: Brajabandhu Pradhan, Senthil T. Kumar, Jessica Wagner, et al.; last authors: Frederic Rousseau, Joost Schymkowitz

Results support two non-exclusive mechanisms: transient interactions redirecting Aβ folding or partial medin incorporation into fibril assembly.

item.title: One-pot fabrication of high-strength Janus hydrogel for wet tissue hemostasis and intestinal/intrauterine anti-adhesion
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72468-8

Existing Janus hydrogels struggle to combine high-strength, asymmetric adhesion, and simple fabrication. Here, the authors develop a high-strength Janus hydrogel with bilateral asymmetric adhesion for wet tissue hemostasis and intestinal/intrauterine anti-adhesion.
item.dc:title: One-pot fabrication of high-strength Janus hydrogel for wet tissue hemostasis and intestinal/intrauterine anti-adhesion

Here, the authors develop a high-strength Janus hydrogel with bilateral asymmetric adhesion for wet tissue hemostasis and intestinal/intrauterine anti-adhesion.
